_doc: Content-hash pins for the reviewed FIRST-PARTY plugin catalogue. Each value is the sha256 of the installed package source (identical to the registry package 'checksum'). NOTE ON SCOPE: these installed sources are thin first-party connectors that re-export reviewed module code shipped INSIDE the Brain's own package, e.g. 'from dreamlayer.plugins.openlibrary import openlibrary_plugin'. So the pin guarantees the INSTALLED package (which lives in the user-writable plugins dir) was not swapped or tampered; the delegated module is part of the Brain's own trusted code base and shares its integrity (editing it requires write access to the Brain's package, which is already a full compromise). This is the RUNTIME half of the curated-registry trust model keys.json describes: a first-party plugin earns in-process execution because its installed bytes match a hash we reviewed here — NOT because of a self-declared 'official' flag (attacker-settable) and NOT via a private signing key (none to custody, none to leak). A look-alike that borrows a first-party name but ships different code hashes differently, misses the pin, and stays in the isolation jail. Regenerate whenever a first-party plugin's source changes (the drift test test_first_party_pins.py fails loudly if this file and the registry checksums disagree).
